justification: The quoted text appears to be only a document title, not an operative policy clause. By itself, it does not specify any rights, duties, conditions, or scope, so an LLM cannot infer the intended policy content from it alone.

This is a broad mission statement expressing values and priorities, not an operational rule with multiple plausible interpretations. While terms like "best service" are general, they do not create a specific policy ambiguity likely to mislead an LLM about required actions.

The phrase "for travel more than 7 days away" could be misread as applying only to the online Accessible Service Request form or to all listed request methods. An LLM might therefore incorrectly infer whether the 7-day restriction limits just the form option or all assistance requests.

This clause is a straightforward list of service categories covered by the policy. While broad, the items are clear labels and do not create a material ambiguity about what types of accessibility-related topics are included.

The term 'push chair' is lexically ambiguous because it can mean either a wheelchair or a stroller in some varieties of English. An LLM could misread what type of assistance is being offered, even though the surrounding section suggests wheelchair-related service.

The clause clearly states that Delta has specially trained Complaint Resolution Officers at all airports and describes their role in handling disability-related concerns and implementation of policies and procedures. The wording is straightforward and unlikely to cause a meaningful misinterpretation by an LLM agent.
The clause is only a document title and does not state any actual baggage rules, conditions, or obligations. An LLM agent cannot infer the intended policy content from this heading alone.

The clause clearly states the fee amount, applies it to the first checked bag, and specifies the weight condition and directionality ('each way'). There is no evident ambiguity in wording that would likely cause misinterpretation by an LLM agent.

The clause clearly states the fee for a second checked bag on domestic U.S. flights: $55 USD per direction for bags under 50 lbs. While it does not address overweight bags, that is a matter of scope rather than ambiguity in the stated rule.

The clause clearly states the measurement rule: total linear dimensions are calculated as length + width + height, and that sum must not exceed 62 inches (158 cm). An LLM is unlikely to misinterpret the intended policy from this wording.

The clause lists categories with benefits or exceptions but does not specify the exact scope, conditions, or limits of those benefits (especially for 'special allowances' and 'additional benefits'). An LLM could misinterpret what fees are waived, how many bags are covered, or whether benefits overlap across categories.

The phrase "with checked bag purchase" can attach ambiguously to the entire entitlement or only to the personal item, making it unclear whether both the free carry-on and personal item require purchasing a checked bag. An LLM could misread the scope of that condition and apply it incorrectly.

The clause clearly states that for the listed standard products (Main, Comfort, Premium Select), each bag has a 50 lb. weight limit. The scope and meaning are straightforward in the baggage weight-limit context.

The clause clearly states that Delta One and First Class passengers are subject to a 70 lb. limit for each bag. The referenced passenger categories and the per-piece weight limit are explicit and unlikely to be misread.

The clause is straightforward: it applies to all passengers traveling to or from Key West, Florida, and sets a clear limit of one checked bag. There is no meaningful ambiguity in scope, reference, or condition that would likely cause misinterpretation.

The clause clearly states that no excess baggage of any kind—by weight, size, or number of pieces—is accepted on regional flights to Mexican cities. Its scope and conditions are explicit enough that an LLM is unlikely to misinterpret the intended restriction.

The phrase 'only to Central/South America' conflicts in scope with the later prohibition of Brazil, Mexico, and Chile, because Brazil and Chile are within South America while Mexico is not. An LLM could misread whether the first sentence defines a general allowed region with exceptions, or whether the geography/categories are being used inconsistently.

The clause clearly states that standard checked bag fees may be paid with miles instead of cash, and specifies the allowed timing and channels for doing so. While detailed operational rules may exist elsewhere, the sentence itself does not present a material ambiguity likely to mislead an LLM about the core policy.

This text is only a title identifying the policy document and does not state any actual rule, condition, or entitlement. An LLM agent could misinterpret it by inferring policy content that is not explicitly provided.

The clause leaves key details unspecified, especially what counts as 'applicable fees' and what situations are covered by 'unless otherwise specified.' An LLM could misinterpret the amount of eCredit or the validity period because those conditions are not fully defined here.

The clause is straightforward: it identifies the eligible ticket types, the timing condition (before flight departure), and the refund outcome (full refund to the original payment method). While 'Extra' may be a product name, in context it functions as a defined ticket type rather than creating genuine ambiguity.

The clause mixes a broad default rule ('in most cases') with multiple exceptions and then adds a separate statement about 'Some Delta Main Classic and above tickets,' making it unclear whether that second sentence is an additional exception, a subset of the first refundable-ticket exception, or an independent regional carve-out. An LLM could misread which tickets are exempt from cancellation fees and under what geographic limits.

The clause combines two different timing conditions—'within 24 hours of booking' and 'by midnight the day after purchase or departure, whichever comes first'—without clearly stating how they interact. An LLM could misread whether both deadlines must be satisfied or whether the latter replaces the 24-hour window.

The phrase 'may request refunds or 5-year eCredits for unflown portions and upgrades' has unclear scope: it can be read as applying to both unflown flight segments and upgrades, or only to upgrades. An LLM could misread whether refunds/eCredits cover the entire disrupted itinerary, just unused portions, or upgrades separately.

The clause clearly distinguishes refund processing times by payment method: 7 business days for credit or debit card purchases and about 20 calendar days for other payment methods. The use of 'typically' and 'approximately' adds flexibility but does not create meaningful ambiguity about the intended policy.

The list structure makes it unclear whether 'without disruptions' modifies only 'non-refundable tickets' or all listed categories. An LLM could misread the scope and incorrectly allow or deny refunds for already-used tickets or group reservations depending on disruptions.
This is only a document title identifying the policy, not an operative clause with conditions or requirements. It does not present multiple plausible interpretations of policy meaning.

The clause clearly states the allowance: each passenger may bring one carry-on bag and one personal item at no charge. The phrase about storing items during flight is descriptive and does not materially change the baggage entitlement.

The clause presents two size limits that can be read either conjunctively (bags must satisfy both the 45 linear inch total and the 22 x 14 x 9 per-dimension cap) or as alternative ways of expressing the same restriction. An LLM could misinterpret whether a bag that meets one limit but not the other is allowed.

The clause lists examples of acceptable personal items but does not clearly state whether a passenger may bring one item total from any listed category or one item from each bullet. The phrase 'Similar-sized items fitting under the seat' also leaves the acceptance criteria open-ended without defining what counts as similar-sized.

The clause appears to omit a verb or noun phrase (e.g., 'items are allowed as carry-on' or 'may be carried on'), making the operative rule incomplete. An LLM could misread whether these items are permitted in addition to the standard allowance or are themselves the only carry-on items covered.

The clause is unclear about the scope of the size and bag limits: an LLM could read it as each liquid/gel/aerosol/paste item being limited to 3.4 oz and all such items fitting within one quart-size bag, or as the total amount collectively being limited to 3.4 oz in one bag. The attachment of 'in one single quart-size, zip-top bag' also creates scope ambiguity over whether it modifies each item or the entire set of items.
The clause is a straightforward enumerated list of prohibited items. While some item labels are broad, the prohibition itself is clear and unlikely to cause an LLM to misread the policy's intent.

The phrase 'larger bags' is relative and does not specify a clear size threshold, which could lead an LLM to guess what qualifies as too large for onboard allowance. 'Personal items' may also be interpreted variably without an explicit definition in the clause itself.

The provided text is only a document title, not an operative policy clause, so it does not specify any rule, condition, or action. An LLM agent could not reliably infer the intended policy from this alone.

The phrase 'up to 24 Hours Before Your Flight' does not clearly specify whether 24 hours is the earliest time check-in opens, the latest recommended time, or an allowed window ending at departure. An LLM could misread the permitted check-in timeframe because the lower and upper bounds are not explicitly stated.

The clause states that international travelers need 'additional documentation' but does not specify what documents are required. It is also unclear whether 'can be provided via the mobile app' means the documents themselves may be submitted there or only that information about them is available there.

The clause clearly describes online check-in access methods, timing, acceptable identifiers, and boarding pass retrieval options. While operational details may be omitted, the wording is not ambiguous in a way likely to cause misinterpretation of the policy.

The clause clearly states that downloading the app enables check-in or login, and that users who log in receive automatic check-in 24 hours before departure plus Today Mode features. While slightly compressed, it does not present a genuine ambiguity likely to cause misinterpretation of the policy.

The clause lists kiosk capabilities but omits important limits and conditions, such as whether all listed actions are always available, who is eligible, and whether baggage addition or service requests are subject to route, timing, or fare restrictions. An LLM could incorrectly infer these functions are universally available at airport kiosks.

The clause states that in-person assistance is available and mentions designated Sky Priority lanes, but it does not specify whether all elite members and premium cabin passengers may use those lanes at every airport or under what conditions. In the context of online check-in options, the relationship between desk availability and eligibility is underspecified.

The clause leaves key coverage details unspecified: 'most U.S. airports' does not identify which airports are included, and 'may require indoor processing' does not state when or under what size/weight conditions this applies. An LLM could overgeneralize availability or mishandle oversized-baggage exceptions.

The clause is only a title or heading and does not state any operative policy, obligation, or condition. Without additional text, an LLM cannot determine the intended customer service commitment from this clause alone.

This clause is a straightforward introductory statement identifying that the plan contains 12 commitments and specifying the flight scope as to, from, or within the U.S. It does not present a meaningful ambiguity likely to cause misinterpretation by an LLM agent.

The clause leaves key conditions underspecified, especially what 'when available elsewhere' means and under what circumstances Delta must disclose or offer the lowest fare. It also does not clearly define how 'matching fares to your specific travel needs' limits or overrides the lowest-fare commitment.

The clause clearly states a 30-minute timeframe starting from when the airline becomes aware of the change, and lists the channels used to provide updates. While operational details could vary, the policy language itself is sufficiently clear for an LLM to interpret the obligation.

The clause leaves key policy details unspecified, especially what qualifies as 'reasonable expenses' and whether the 15-30 hour international window depends on route, destination, or other conditions. An LLM could misapply the compensation rule or the applicable delivery timeframe because those criteria are not defined here.

The phrase 'for 24-hours after the reservation is made' is scope-ambiguous: it could mean the cancellation right lasts up to 24 hours after booking, or that the ticket can be cancelled and then takes 24 hours to process. An LLM could misread the time limit applying to the refund process rather than the eligibility window.

The clause is grammatically unclear and omits key details such as what event starts the 7-business-day clock for credit card refunds, while the cash/check timing is tied to Delta's initial receipt. This missing parallel trigger could lead an LLM to infer different start points for the two refund methods.

The clause clearly lists accommodations Delta provides and specifies the age range for children traveling alone. While it lacks operational detail, the basic meaning is straightforward and unlikely to be misinterpreted by an LLM agent.

The qualifier "when safety conditions permit" has unclear scope: it could apply to all listed provisions, or only to some of them such as leaving seats to access lavatories or receive medical attention. An LLM might therefore misread when the airline must provide each essential need during a tarmac delay.

The clause lists several actions but omits key conditions and limits for when each applies, especially whether compensation, rebooking, and hotel accommodations apply to volunteers, involuntarily denied passengers, or both. This missing eligibility detail could cause an LLM to overgeneralize the benefits.

The clause straightforwardly states that the listed policy information can be accessed via delta.com and by phone. It does not present a meaningful ambiguity likely to cause misinterpretation by an LLM agent.

The clause omits important conditions and scope, such as whether notifications are guaranteed only when the airline has valid contact information, what kinds of itinerary changes are covered, and what happens if a change occurs less than one hour before departure. An LLM could misread it as an unconditional promise to notify all changes at least one hour before departure.

The timing requirements are stated clearly: written complaints get acknowledgment within 30 days, substantive responses within 60 days, and disability complaints are resolved within 30 days. While 'substantive response' could vary in practice, the clause does not present a genuine structural ambiguity likely to mislead an LLM about the deadlines.

The clause strings together several benefits and conditions without clearly marking which conditions apply to which remedies. An LLM could misread whether 'for delays over 3 hours within Delta's control' applies only to hotel/meals or also to rebooking and discretionary compensation.

This is only a document title, not an operative policy clause, so it does not specify any actual rules, conditions, or scope for flight changes or rebooking. An LLM agent could not reliably infer the intended policy from this text alone.

The phrase "excluding Basic experiences" is lexically unclear because it does not use standard fare terminology and could be misread as excluding certain travel itineraries or customer experiences rather than a specific fare class (e.g., Basic Economy). This could cause an LLM to misapply the exception.

The phrase "these regions" lacks a clear antecedent in the clause itself, so an agent may not know which geographic regions are being excluded. This makes the scope of "travel outside these regions" unclear when determining the applicable change fee.

The phrase "Travel Experience tier" is not clearly defined and could be interpreted in multiple ways, such as cabin class, fare brand, or ancillary service bundle. An LLM agent could misread what exactly remains unchanged after the flight modification.

The term "Extra/Refundable Tickets" is unclear because "Extra" is not a standard ticket category and could be interpreted in multiple ways. An LLM might misread which ticket types the fee-free change rule applies to.

The phrase 'domestic U.S. travel or international travel originating in the U.S. and Canada' is unclear in scope: 'originating in the U.S. and Canada' could modify only international travel, and 'in the U.S. and Canada' may be read as requiring origin in both countries or either country. An LLM could misread which itineraries qualify for no change fees.

The clause gives a straightforward sequence of steps for modifying a booking online and does not present a meaningful ambiguity in scope, reference, or conditions. While it does not cover every possible exception, the instruction itself is clear enough that an LLM agent is unlikely to misinterpret the intended process.

The exception list can be read as modifying either the ticket-purchase condition, the handling charge, or both. In particular, 'except when changes occur via delta.com' is unclear because the base rule is about how the ticket was purchased, while the exception is about how the change is processed.

The clause clearly states that for reissued tickets, all travel must be completed within one year of the original purchase date. The reference point ('original purchase date') and requirement ('complete travel') are sufficiently explicit for policy interpretation.
The clause is only a title and does not state any actual baggage rules, limits, or conditions. An LLM agent cannot infer the intended policy content from this heading alone.

The clause is a general overview statement and clearly says baggage allowance is determined by either the weight or piece concept based on route and fare type. While it lacks operational detail, it does not create a meaningful ambiguity about the policy's intended interpretation.

The phrase "except those to/from the Americas and Africa" has unclear scope: it can be read as excluding routes to/from the Americas and also all routes involving Africa, or only routes to/from both regions under a combined exception. An LLM could misapply the policy depending on how it groups "to/from" with the coordinated regions.

The clause lists baggage allowances by fare class but does not specify whether the values are per piece, total checked baggage weight, or per passenger. The exception '30 kg from Australia/New Zealand' is also underspecified because it could refer to origin only and does not clarify whether both countries and all routes from them are covered.

The clause allows multiple bags 'up to total weight allowance' but does not specify the actual total weight allowance within the clause itself, so an agent cannot determine the permitted combined weight without additional context. This missing parameter could lead to misapplication of the bag limit.

The clause states a general size limit and a special 'Dammam Airport limit' but does not specify the condition under which the 215 cm limit applies (e.g., departures, arrivals, transfers, or all baggage at that airport). An LLM could misapply the stricter limit because the triggering scope is left unstated.

The clause clearly assigns per-bag weight limits by cabin class: 23 kg for Economy and 32 kg for Premium, Business, and First. The parenthetical labels unambiguously specify which classes each limit applies to.

The clause clearly maps each listed fare category to a specific number of allowed baggage pieces within the stated Piece Concept section. The category grouping "Premium Economy and above" is a standard fare-tier expression and is unlikely to cause material misinterpretation in this context.

The phrase "total dimensions" is underspecified because it does not explicitly state whether it means the sum of length + width + height. An LLM could misread it as a limit on any single dimension or as a generic size cap rather than a summed measurement.

The exception phrase '(or 215 cm from Dammam)' is underspecified because it does not clearly state whether 'from Dammam' means departures only, all itineraries involving Dammam, or a route-specific measurement rule. This could lead an LLM to misapply the size cutoff for checkability.

The clause leaves key details unspecified, especially whether delayed delivery applies to all bags or only the excess bags beyond 15, and under what exact conditions 'may result' is triggered. This missing information could lead an LLM to infer different operational rules.

The clause states that Platinum/Gold/Silver Skywards members receive an extra allowance, but it does not specify the amount or nature of that allowance. An LLM agent could misinterpret how much extra baggage is permitted or whether the allowance applies uniformly across all weight-concept routes.

This is only a document title, not an operative policy clause, so it does not specify any actual cancellation or change rules. An LLM agent could not infer the intended policy details from this text alone.

The clause makes refund eligibility depend on the ticket's fare conditions but does not specify what those conditions are. An LLM agent could wrongly generalize that a full refund is broadly available, when it is actually limited by unstated fare-specific rules.

The clause does not specify which tickets are non-refundable versus which are refundable with cancellation charges, nor how an agent should determine the applicable fare conditions. This missing decision criterion could cause an LLM to guess the cancellation outcome for a given ticket.

The clause does not specify which bookings qualify for online changes or which specific changes incur charges. An LLM could not reliably determine eligibility or fees without the missing fare-condition details.

The clause bundles several limitations, but key conditions are underspecified, especially 'may be eligible for changes based on fare conditions' and whether the 72-hour rule applies only to online changes or to all changes. An LLM could misread the scope of eligibility and processing channels because the policy does not fully define those limits here.

The pronoun "These" could refer to either the credit notes (EMDs) or the refund eligibility/outcome generally, which may affect how an agent interprets what can be applied to future travel or redeemed. The clause also leaves unclear whether redemption through the refund request form converts the EMD to cash or is simply another way to use the credit.

The phrase '12-24 hours in advance depending on destination' has unclear scope: it could modify when the new bookings must be made, when the cancellation occurs, or the conditions under which changed flights trigger cancellation. This could lead an LLM to misread the timing requirement in the policy.

The quoted text is only a title, not an actual policy clause, so it provides no operative rule for baggage handling, limits, or exceptions. An LLM agent would lack the necessary information to infer the intended policy.
The clause clearly states that each ticket includes one personal item. In the given section context, there is no genuine ambiguity likely to cause misinterpretation by an LLM agent.

The clause clearly states the maximum allowed dimensions for a personal item and explicitly includes handles, wheels, and straps in the measurement. There is no meaningful ambiguity likely to cause misinterpretation by an LLM agent.

The clause gives examples of acceptable personal items but does not state whether the list is exhaustive or merely illustrative. An LLM could misinterpret whether similar items not listed are also allowed.

The clause clearly states that the passenger's personal item size will be checked at boarding. While it does not specify exact size limits, there is no clear linguistic ambiguity in how the requirement itself should be interpreted.

The clause does not specify which 'allowed dimensions' it refers to in this section or what the additional charge amount/conditions are. An LLM could misinterpret whether this applies specifically to personal items, all baggage types, or a different dimensional standard.

The baggage size and weight limits are stated clearly, and the included examples do not materially alter the rule. The pricing sentence is ancillary marketing language rather than a policy condition, so it is unlikely to cause misinterpretation of the carry-on requirements.

The phrase 'This premium option' is underspecified within the clause and could refer either to the general Board First product or specifically to the carry-on/overhead-bin guarantee. An LLM could misread which exact option is being said to guarantee bin space versus merely permit Group 1 boarding.

The clause says bags can be purchased up to 60 minutes before both domestic and international flights, making the parenthetical distinction pointless and leaving unclear whether a different international cutoff was intended. It also says the service accommodates sporting equipment while stating a maximum size and weight, without clarifying whether sporting equipment is subject to the same limits or exceptions.

The clause is unclear about what exactly may be upgraded through the website or mobile app: whether both weight ranges are available in both channels, or whether channel availability varies by upgrade type. An LLM could misread the scope of 'through the website or mobile app' as applying differently to the listed weight options or the fee condition.

The quoted text is only a document title, not an operative policy clause, so it lacks the substantive rule, conditions, or scope needed to determine meaning. An LLM could not infer the intended policy from this fragment alone.

The clause is broad and densely drafted, but its intent is clear: the Contract of Carriage incorporates the listed terms and applies to all Frontier-issued tickets and related transportation regardless of seller or use status. Any awkward phrasing does not create a genuine interpretive ambiguity likely to mislead an LLM about the policy’s scope.

This is a straightforward definitional clause. While 'share' is informal and the parenthetical is broad, the intended meaning of a codeshare arrangement is clear enough that an LLM is unlikely to misinterpret the policy.

This is a straightforward definitional clause describing a codeshare arrangement: one airline applies its code to a flight operated by another and sells tickets for it. The references and conditions are clear enough that an LLM is unlikely to misinterpret the intended meaning.

This is a straightforward definitional clause: it states that 'Fare Rules' means the rules and requirements associated with a ticket. While broad, it does not present a genuine ambiguity of scope, reference, or condition that would likely cause misinterpretation.

This is a definitional clause that clearly states no-show cancellation occurs when a passenger fails to check in or fails to board within the required times. While 'required times' depends on elsewhere-defined deadlines, that is normal cross-reference rather than a genuine ambiguity in this clause.

The clause does not define what event triggers 'automatic cancellation,' so an agent may misread what kind of cancellation is being described. 'Subsequent flights' can also be interpreted differently, such as all later segments versus only later segments on the same ticketed journey.

The clause clearly states a condition and consequence: if anyone other than the named passenger presents the ticket, the ticket becomes void and is treated as a No-Show Cancellation. The referents and scope are sufficiently explicit for policy interpretation.

This is a standard legal definition that tracks regulatory language and clearly lists three alternative qualifying conditions. The cross-reference to 14 CFR 382.5 adds specificity rather than creating genuine ambiguity.

The definition is straightforward: a standby passenger is boarded only if a seat is available at departure time and only after passengers with confirmed reservations have boarded. While compactly phrased, it does not present a genuine ambiguity likely to cause misinterpretation.

This is a definitional clause that clearly states a ticket is the record of agreement for air transportation, including electronic tickets, and identifies the governing sources of terms and conditions. While broad, it does not present a genuine ambiguity likely to cause misinterpretation by an LLM agent.

The phrase 'for the following reasons, in which case Frontier will provide a refund' can be read as applying the refund/limited-liability consequence either to all listed refusal/removal actions or only to some subset tied to specific reasons. An LLM could mis-scope whether the refund condition attaches broadly to any refusal/removal under this section or narrowly to particular enumerated grounds.

The clause is a bare heading or fragment rather than a complete rule, so it does not specify the actual condition or consequence being imposed. An LLM could not reliably infer whether it means a safety assistant is not entitled to a seat, that transport is refused if no seat is available for the assistant, or something else.

The clause clearly states that Frontier may refuse transportation for listed reasons and that, in those cases, no refund to the original form of payment is due. It is introductory and depends on the following enumerated reasons, but that dependency does not create genuine ambiguity about the rule stated here.

“Government Direction” is only a heading or fragment, not a complete operative clause, so it does not specify the actual rule, condition, or action required. An LLM could not reliably infer what transportation refusal or special condition is intended from this text alone.

The clause is straightforward in this context: refusal to produce government-issued identification is a stated ground for refusal to transport. While implementation details could vary, the core condition is clear and unlikely to be misread by an LLM agent.

The conjunction 'or' creates uncertainty about whether refusal of either action alone is sufficient for refusal to transport, or whether the policy is targeting refusal to do both. An LLM could misread the scope of the negation across 'create a MyFrontier account' and 'have a credit or debit card on file with Frontier.'

The phrase is only a heading or fragment and does not state the operative rule, conditions, or consequences. An LLM could not reliably infer what happens if a passenger fails to check in or appear without additional text.

The phrase "Special Medical Requirements" is only a heading or fragment, not a complete rule. It does not specify what requirements apply, to whom, or under what conditions, so an LLM could not reliably infer the intended policy.

The clause consists only of the term "Qualified Individual with a Disability" without any accompanying definition or operative rule. An LLM could not determine the intended criteria, scope, or consequences from this fragment alone.

The clause is direct and categorical: Frontier will not transport prisoners under any circumstances. The subject, action, and scope are explicit, leaving little room for misinterpretation by an LLM agent.

The phrase "fully clothed" is inherently vague and can be interpreted differently depending on context, making it unclear what minimum attire is required. An LLM could misapply the rule because the policy does not define what clothing standard counts as sufficient.

The phrase "Malodorous Condition" is only a heading or fragment and does not specify the operative rule, threshold, or consequence. Without surrounding text, an LLM cannot determine what condition qualifies or what action the carrier may take.

The quoted text is only a heading or fragment, not a complete operative rule, so it does not specify what action, condition, or consequence applies. An LLM could not reliably infer the intended policy from this clause alone.

The phrase "Refusal or Inability to Sit" is only a heading-like fragment and does not state the operative rule, conditions, or consequences. An LLM could misinterpret what conduct is covered or what action the airline may take because the clause is incomplete without the accompanying text.

The phrase "Failure to Follow Instructions" is only a heading or fragment, not a complete rule. Without the operative text, an LLM cannot determine whose instructions, what kind of failure, or what consequences the policy intends.

The phrase is only a heading or fragment and does not state the operative rule, conditions, or consequences. Without the surrounding text, an LLM cannot determine whether such use is prohibited, allowed under exceptions, or grounds for refusal to transport.

The phrase is only a heading or label and does not state what conduct is prohibited, what counts as a violation, or what consequence applies. An LLM could not reliably infer the operative rule from this fragment alone.

The clause is broad but not linguistically ambiguous: it plainly grants Frontier discretion to refuse to sell transportation to any person. An LLM may find it sweeping, but the intended policy meaning is straightforward.

The clause hinges on subjective terms such as "in Frontier's sole judgment," "unable to sit," and "occupying all or a portion of the adjacent seats," which do not provide clear operational criteria. An LLM could misinterpret when the additional-seat requirement is triggered because the threshold for encroachment or inability to fit is not objectively defined.

The clause clearly states that items will not be removed from the aircraft to accommodate allergies and that the airline cannot provide peanut-free or chemical-free environments. While 'items' is somewhat broad, the overall policy intent is straightforward and unlikely to be misinterpreted by an LLM agent.

The clause clearly states the triggering conditions: if Frontier diverts a flight or returns to the gate because a passenger needs medical attention or because of that passenger's conduct, that passenger may have to reimburse Frontier's resulting costs. While terms like 'may be required' preserve discretion, they do not create a genuine ambiguity about the policy's basic meaning.

The clause is straightforward: transportation entitlement requires presentation of a valid electronic ticket. Terms like 'valid' and 'electronic ticket' are standard in this context and do not create a meaningful ambiguity likely to mislead an LLM about the policy.

The clause clearly states that Frontier does not issue paper tickets on its own ticket stock. While 'ticket stock' is industry-specific, the policy meaning is straightforward and unlikely to be misinterpreted by an LLM agent.

The phrase "in the order in which they are issued" is lexically ambiguous because "issued" could refer to coupon/flight-segment sequencing, ticket purchase chronology, or document issuance events. An LLM could misread which ordering constraint governs when the ticket may be honored.

The clause lists prohibited practices but does not define what each named ticketing practice means within the clause itself. An LLM agent could misinterpret the scope or application of these terms without explicit definitions or incorporated references.
The clause clearly states that only the individual whose name appears on the ticket may use it. Its meaning is straightforward and unlikely to be misinterpreted by an LLM agent.

The phrase 'transport of items of baggage' is lexically unclear because baggage is ordinarily checked or carried under a passenger's ticket, not itself 'transported' via a separate ticket. An LLM could misread this as banning purchase of tickets when traveling with baggage, rather than banning buying tickets for baggage as if it were a passenger.

The clause is only a section heading ('A. Period of Validity') and does not state any operative rule, timeframe, or conditions. Without accompanying text, an LLM cannot determine what validity period applies or how it affects ticket use or itinerary changes.

The clause leaves key terms underspecified, especially 'certain other carrier charges,' which does not identify what additional deductions apply. An LLM could also be unsure whether 'retained for 365 days' refers only to the credit's usability period or some broader validity condition.

The clause clearly states that, unless law requires otherwise, Frontier has no duty to reschedule passengers who cancel or to provide a refund or credit for unused tickets. The exception and the two denied obligations are expressed plainly enough that an LLM is unlikely to misread the intended policy.

The exception phrase 'except for tickets purchased for travel within 7 days (168 hours) of purchase' can be read as modifying either the 24-hour cancellation/refund right or the broader refund rule, creating uncertainty about whether those close-in tickets are entirely excluded from the 24-hour refund window. An LLM could misapply the policy by granting or denying a refund depending on how it attaches that exception.

The clause clearly states that a published fare is valid only for travel between the specific origin and destination airports for which that fare was published. Its terms are straightforward and do not present a meaningful ambiguity likely to mislead an LLM agent.

The clause clearly states that fare amounts may change at any time before ticket issuance and become fixed once a ticket is issued. Its timing condition and scope are straightforward, so an LLM is unlikely to misinterpret the intended policy.
The clause is straightforward: a fee is charged for every checked bag. There is no clear lexical, scopal, referential, or structural ambiguity likely to cause misinterpretation by an LLM agent.

The phrase 'their accompanying spouse and children' can be mis-scoped: an agent may read the entitlement as two free bags total for the family unit, or as applying individually to the military member and each accompanying spouse/child. The waiver for overweight and oversize charges on 'the first two free bags' inherits that uncertainty.

The clause leaves key terms undefined, especially what qualifies as 'Essential Infant/Child Items.' Without a clear list or criteria, an LLM could misjudge which child-related items are exempt from baggage charges and allowance limits.

The clause states clear maximum limits for checked baggage size and weight, with both imperial and metric units provided. 'Linear dimension' is a standard baggage term meaning the total of length, width, and height, so the limits are unlikely to be misread by an LLM.

The exception phrase "with the exception of assistive devices or musical instruments" can be read as applying to both limits (weight and linear size) or only to the immediately preceding size restriction. An LLM could therefore misinterpret whether assistive devices or musical instruments may exceed both thresholds or just one of them.

The clause clearly states that Frontier must make reasonable efforts to transport passengers and baggage, while expressly disclaiming any guarantee as to schedules, flight times, aircraft types, seat assignments, and similar operational details. Its meaning is straightforward and unlikely to cause an LLM to misread the intended policy.
